steps.prompt: Which word is the finite verb?

hints.Hierosolymam: 'Hierosolymam' is a proper noun meaning 'Jerusalem' in the accusative
hints.none of these: There is a verb — 'ascendimus' is the 1st person plural form

hints.to send: 'To send' is 'mittere' — 'ascendimus' comes from 'ascendere' (to go up)
hints.to light/kindle: 'To light' is 'accendere' — similar sounding but 'ascendere' means 'to ascend'
hints.to climb down: 'To climb down' is 'descendere' — 'ascendere' means 'to go up'

steps.reveal: ascendimus = we are going up / we ascend

hints.perfect indicative: 'Ascendimus' can be perfect (we ascended), but with 'ecce' announcing imminent action, present is more natural
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'ascendemus' — 'ascendimus' is present or perfect
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'ascendamus' — 'ascendimus' is indicative

hints.accendo, accendere, accendi, accensum: These are the parts of 'accendere' (to kindle/light)
hints.descendo, descendere, descendi, descensu: These are the parts of 'descendere' (to descend) — the opposite
hints.scando, scandere, scandi, scansum: These are the parts of 'scandere' (to climb) — the simple verb

steps.reveal: Hierosolymam = to Jerusalem (acc., goal of motion)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Hierosolyma' — the -am ending marks accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'Hierosolyma' or 'Hierosolymis' — 'Hierosolymam' is accusative
hints.dative: Dative would be 'Hierosolymae' — 'Hierosolymam' indicates direction toward

steps.prompt: Why is there no preposition before 'Hierosolymam'?
steps.target: names of cities use accusative without a preposition for 'place to which'
choices: names of cities use accusative without a preposition for 'place to which'
the preposition 'ad' is implied but omitted by scribal error

hints.the preposition 'ad' is implied but omit: This is standard Latin grammar — city names take bare accusative for destination
hints.'Hierosolymam' is actually ablative: 'Hierosolymam' with -am is clearly accusative — city names use this for 'to'
hints.interjections replace prepositions: 'Ecce' does not replace a preposition — the bare accusative is the rule for city names

hints.adverb: 'Ecce' does not modify a verb — it is an exclamatory interjection
hints.conjunction: A conjunction joins clauses — 'ecce' introduces with emphasis
hints.preposition: A preposition would govern a case — 'ecce' is an interjection

hints.1st person singular: 1st singular would be 'ascendo' — 'ascendimus' ends in -mus (1st plural)

translation: Behold, we are going up to Jerusalem
